evidence_excerpt: At age 9, she developed severe esophageal dysmotility requiring balloon dilations at ages 10 and 14; alacrima persisted and glucocorticoid replacement continued, though hypoglycemia control improved.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Ongoing alacrima requiring artificial tears (stable, no corneal ulcers); achalasia managed with dilations and soft diet; migraines controlled; no hypoglycemia episodes after age 9.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: After starting maintenance hydrocortisone, the patient improved and regained her energy level; ACTH normalized.
Several hypoglycemic episodes down to 30 mg/dL, with 2 seizures around age 6 during viral illness.
Notable weight gain and elevated hemoglobin A1c (5.1% to 6.1%) between ages 7.5 and 8.5, attributed to higher steroid doses.
No more severe hypoglycemia (<50 mg/dL) after age 7; later no episodes at all after implementing glucose gel plan.
After adding glucose gel and lowering steroid doses, hemoglobin A1c improved from 6.2% to 5.4% and no hypoglycemia recorded after age 9.
At age 9, she developed severe esophageal dysmotility confirmed by esophagram and manometry.
Required esophageal balloon dilation at age 10.
Required repeat esophageal balloon dilation at age 14.
Post-dilation, she is able to tolerate a soft diet.

notes_on_uncertainty: Index time approximated to the initial diagnosis of Allgrove syndrome and initiation of hydrocortisone at age ~3. Exact dates are not provided; time periods are inferred from ages reported. Functional status and symptom burden are inferred from narrative descriptions of school performance, diet tolerance, and ongoing management.
